policy_statement: Improve energy-related competence of housing cooperative decision-makers through training and case presentations on energy efficiency and renewable energy.
mechanism_description: Training and case presentations improve housing cooperative decision-makers’ competence on energy efficiency and renewable energy, helping enable choices that reduce building energy use and increase clean energy uptake.
primary_causal_pathway: Capacity building/training → ↑ adoption of efficiency + renewables → ↓ fossil energy use in buildings → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changes: Improves the competence and decision processes of housing cooperative boards/decision-makers regarding energy efficiency measures and renewable energy procurement/installation.  
(2) Mediating process: Better-informed decisions overcome informational/transaction-cost barriers, leading to higher uptake and quality of retrofits (insulation, controls, HVAC upgrades) and clean energy (e.g., solar PV, renewable electricity contracts), reducing delivered energy and carbon intensity.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower operational building energy demand and/or cleaner energy supply, producing net reductions in CO₂e emissions from building operations.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption and associated combustion-related GHG emissions due to more frequent/better energy-efficiency and clean-energy investment decisions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Training/capacity-building changes decisions → increases adoption of efficiency/renewables → reduces fossil energy use and thus GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: The primary intended and largest climate effect is emissions reduction, not adaptation (reducing hazard exposure) or resource efficiency as an end in itself; efficiency here is a means to cut energy-related emissions in buildings.  
3) Edge cases applied: If training leads mainly to energy cost savings without measurable emissions impact, it still primarily targets emissions via energy reduction; if it focused on resilience (e.g., backup power for outages), it could shift toward Adaptation, but that is not indicated in this mechanism description.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy using this mechanism—training building decision-makers to enable efficiency/renewables choices—primarily drives operational emissions reductions in the buildings sector.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether the downstream impact is mostly efficiency vs renewables, and the magnitude, depends on policy design and uptake evidence at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the training is primarily about comfort/health or resilience (e.g., overheating preparedness, outage planning) with minimal energy/emissions effects, classification could shift; similarly, if it is purely “market development” without clear link to emissions outcomes, confidence would be lower. Otherwise straightforward.
key_indicators: “competences … will be improved”; “will receive training”; “Case presentations … will be prepared”; “energy efficiency”; “renewable energy”
co_benefits: Lower energy bills/operating costs; improved indoor comfort; potential health benefits from better building performance
instance_edge_case_notes: Classified as information-disclosure because the primary lever is training/case-study knowledge transfer; could also be seen as a planning-strategy/capacity-building program, but no formal plan, targets, or investment components are specified.
